synonyms: SOF|Study of Osteoporotic Fractures (SOF) Frailty Index|Study of Osteoporotic Fractures Index|Study of Osteoporotic Fractures [SOF] Index
definition: A frailty index consisting of 3 components: weight loss, the subject's inability to rise from a chair 5 times without using their arms, and reduced energy level.

A self-report measure of perceived ability to perform various mathematical tasks and preference for the use of numerical versus prose information. The 8-item scale contains no mathematics questions and has no correct or incorrect answers. Instead, it consists of 4 questions asking respondents to assess their numerical ability in different contexts and 4 questions asking them to state their preferences for the presentation of numerical and probabilistic information. (Fagerlin, et al., Medical Decision Making, 2007: 27: 672-680)

A standardized rating scale developed by Leonard Handelsman and colleagues in 1987 to evaluate the severity of an individual's opiate withdrawal signs and symptoms. This instrument contains 16 items or symptoms that pertain to opiate withdrawal, which are completed by the individual. Each item is rated on a scale of 0 to 4 with 0 being not at all and 4 being extremely.

A standardized 30-item self-report questionnaire developed by William M. Reynolds in 1987 that utilizes a seven-point rating scale to assess the frequency of suicidal thoughts in adolescents grades 10 to 12.

A standardized 15-item self-report questionnaire developed by William M. Reynolds in 1987 that utilizes a seven-point rating scale to assess the frequency of suicidal thoughts in adolescents grades 7 to 9.

A questionnaire developed by Zilliox et al. in 2011 that consists of 11 items for women and 12 items for men, and utilizes a five-point rating scale to assess the severity of autonomic symptoms in patients with neuropathy.

preferred_term: Survey of Food Allergy Anxiety Child Report
synonyms: SOFAA-C|Scale of Food Allergy Anxiety Child Report|Survey of Food Allergy Anxiety Child Report (SOFAA-C)
definition: A 21-item self-rated questionnaire that utilizes a five-point rating scale to assess food allergy anxiety and related anxious avoidance behaviors over the past week in children and adolescents aged 8 to 18 years.

preferred_term: Survey of Food Allergy Anxiety Parent Report
synonyms: SOFAA-P|Scale of Food Allergy Anxiety Parent Report|Survey of Food Allergy Anxiety Parent Report (SOFAA-P)
definition: A 21-item self-rated questionnaire that utilizes a five-point rating scale to assess a parent's perception of their child's food allergy anxiety and related anxious avoidance behaviors over the past week.

A standardized rating scale developed by Aaron Smith in 1982, which is used to identify individuals with neurological impairment, as well as to assess divided attention, visual scanning and motor speed. This functional test measures the time to pair abstract symbols with specific numbers with a 90 second time limit. Scoring of this test is based on the total number of correctly coded items from 0-110 in 90 seconds.

A subtest of the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale, 4th Edition that measures processing speed, short-term visual memory, visual-motor coordination, cognitive flexibility, visual discrimination, psychomotor speed, and speed of mental operation. The subject scans a search group and indicates whether one of the symbols in the target group matches.

A subtest of the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children that measures visual-perceptual and decision-making speed, as well as short-term visual memory, visual-motor coordination, inhibitory control, visual discrimination, psychomotor speed, sustained attention, and concentration.

A 90-item self-report symptom inventory designed to measure psychological symptoms and psychological distress for individuals 13 years and older. It is intended to measure symptom intensity on nine different subscales.
A patient reported questionnaire composed of rating scales developed to measure the degree of distress experienced by the patient for specific symptoms.

A standardized survey developed and copyrighted by Ronald Friend and Robert Bennett that is published in the journal Arthritis Research and Therapy. This instrument was developed along with the Revised Fibromyalgia Questionnaire (FIQR) and was intended for use in assessing symptoms associated with rheumatoid arthritis and systemic lupus erythematous. There are three sections including function, overall impact and symptoms with a total of 21 items. Each item is rated on a 0-10 scale with 0 being none and 10 being the most severe.

A self-report screening tool, originally developed by Tomlinson et al. in 2013, that is used for symptom assessment in children with cancer and pediatric hematopoietic stem cell transplantation recipients.

preferred_term: Symptoms of Major Depressive Disorder Scale v1.0 Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 35-item patient-reported outcome instrument, created by McCarrier et al. in 2015, that utilizes a 7-day recall period and a five-point verbal rating scale to assess symptoms of major depressive disorder.

synonyms: TAPS|TAPS Tool|TAPS Tool Part 1 - Interviewer Version (QSC)|The Tobacco, Alcohol, Prescription Medication and Other Substances Tool - Interviewer Version
definition: An instrument designed to screen and assess adult primary care patients for tobacco, alcohol, prescription drug, and drug use and problems related to their use.

A scale of physical development in children, adolescents and adults based on external primary and secondary sex characteristics, such as the size of the breasts, genitalia, and development of pubic hair.
A physical development stage derived from a Tanner Scale assessment.

A rating scale included in the Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Function (BRIEF) that reflects the ability to keep track of one's problem-solving success or failure, and to identify and correct mistakes during performance of a goal.

synonyms: NHANES Taste and Smell Questionnaire|NHANES Taste and Smell Section
definition: A section of the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) pertaining to taste and smell ability and related health conditions.

A 15-item questionnaire that utilizes a six-point rating scale to assess an individual's attitude toward the use of technology, including their confidence in using technology, the value of using technology, and their perceptions of technology and its usefulness in specific settings.

preferred_term: The Body-Mass Index, Airflow Obstruction, Dyspnea, and Exercise Capacity Index Clinical Classification
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by Celli et al in 2004, which is a multidimensional grading system used to assess the respiratory and systemic expressions of COPD, and to predict the risk of death among patients with COPD. This instrument has a total possible values range from 0 to 10, based on FEV1, body-mass index, the distance walked in six minutes, and the modified MRC dyspnea scale.

preferred_term: The Expanded Prostate Cancer Index Composite Short Form Questionnaire
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by Szymanski et al in 2010, which is used to monitor health-related quality of life outcomes in patients with prostate cancer. This instrument contains five symptom domains (urinary incontinence, urinary irritative/obstructive, sexual, bowel, hormonal) and 26 items, scored from 0 (worst) to 100 (best).

preferred_term: The Glucose Monitoring Satisfaction Survey Version: Type 1 Diabetes Questionnaire
synonyms: GMSS VERSION TYPE 1 DIABETES|GMSS Version Type 1 Diabetes|GMSS01
definition: A 15-item self-administered questionnaire, published by Polonsky et al. in 2015, that utilizes a five-point rating scale to assess patient treatment satisfaction with glucose monitoring devices for type 1 diabetes; the instrument includes four subscales that address openness, emotional burden, behavioral burden, and trust.
A questionnaire designed to assess a patient's perceptions of the degree to which their specific doctor supports the patient's autonomy.
A 2-question food insecurity screening tool that assesses a family's concerns about their food supply and access to food.

preferred_term: The Lansky Play-Performance Status Scale Clinical Classification
definition: A parent-rated questionnaire created by Lansky et al. in 1985 to assess play activity over the past week in children ages 1 to 16 with cancer as an indicator of level of performance. Level of performance is scored from 0 to 100 based on active play, quiet play, degree of physical limitation, and degree of independence; with the interpretation ranging from fully active, normal performance (100), to moderately restricted (50 to 60), to completely disabled (10 or less).

A 13-item, self-administered questionnaire designed to meassure depressive symptoms. The MFQ consists of a series of descriptive phrases regarding how the subject has been feeling or acting recently and is a screening tool for depression in children and young people aged between 6 and 17 years.

preferred_term: The Oswestry Disability Index Version 2.1a Questionnaire
definition: A standardized survey initially developed by John O'Brien in 1976 then published in 1980 by Jeremy Fairbank. This version (2.1a) is copyrighted by Jeremy Fairbank and is used in the assessment and management of spinal disorders. There are 10 items pertaining to a variety of topics including personal care, walking, standing, sleeping and traveling. Each item has six responses.

A standardized survey developed and published by K. Coyne and colleagues in 2002 in the journal Quality of Life Research which is used to evaluate the all symptoms associated with overactive bladder. This instrument contains 33 items pertaining to bladder symptoms occurring during the past week that can be self-administered. Each item is rated on a scale of 1-6 with 1 representing none or not at all and 6 representing a great deal or all the time.

preferred_term: The Overactive Bladder Questionnaire Short Form
definition: A standardized survey developed by K. Coyne and colleagues in 2004 and presented at the Joint Meeting of the International Continence Society and the International Urogynecological Association which is used to evaluate the symptoms associated with overactive bladder. This self-administered, shorter version of the Overactive Bladder Questionnaire (OAB-q) contains 19 items pertaining to bladder symptoms occurring during the past four weeks. Each item is rated on a scale of 1-6 with 1 representing none or not at all and 6 representing a great deal or all the time.

preferred_term: The PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 20-item self-report measure, published in 2013, that is used to assess the 20 Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5) symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), using a 5-point Likert-type intensity scale, ranging from zero (not at all) to four (extremely).

A standardized self-report questionnaire, derived from the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 items and published in 2002, that includes nine items from the diagnostic criteria for MDD in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fourth Edition (DSM-IV) and is used to measure depression severity.

preferred_term: The Physical Activity Readiness Questionnaire for Everyone
synonyms: PAR-Q+|The Physical Activity Readiness Questionnaire for Everyone (PAR-Q+)
definition: A self-administered health screening tool designed to identify individuals who may need medical clearance before starting a group exercise class.

parent_name: Clinical or Research Functional Assessment Tool
definition: A standardized rating scale developed to measure walking ability through gait speed. This functional test is a quantitative mobility and leg function performance test where a study subject is asked to walk 25 feet from a marked starting point. The time is calculated from the initiation to the end of walking when the patient has reached the 25-foot mark. Scoring of this test is based on the average of two completed trials.

preferred_term: Timed Up and Go Functional Test
parent_name: Clinical or Research Functional Assessment Tool
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by Mathias et al in 1986 which is used to test basic mobility skills in frail elderly persons and other populations such as people with arthritis, stroke, and vertigo. This functional test measures the time in seconds for a person to stand up from a standard armchair, walk 3 meters, turn, walk back to the chair, and sit down again. Scoring of this test is based on the average of multiple completed and timed trials.

preferred_term: Toronto Extremity Salvage Score, Lower Extremity
synonyms: Lower Extremity Questionnaire|TESS|TESS Lower|TESS Lower Extremity|Toronto Extremity Salvage Score|Toronto Extremity Salvage Score (TESS) Lower Extremity
definition: A self-administered questionnaire designed to assess physical disability for patients undergoing lower limb salvage sugery for musculoskeletal tumors.

preferred_term: Toronto Extremity Salvage Score, Upper Extremity
synonyms: TESS|TESS Upper|TESS Upper Extremity|Toronto Extremity Salvage Score|Toronto Extremity Salvage Score (TESS) Upper Extremity|Upper Extremity Questionnaire
definition: A self-administered questionnaire designed to assess physical disability for patients undergoing upper limb salvage surgery for musculoskeletal tumors.
A subscale of the Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale that is the average of the symptom scores of all 32 symptoms in the full MSAS instrument.
A method that summarizes individual toxic effects into an overall toxicity burden score.
An index derived from the Food Environment Atlas that calculates for a county the number of fast food restaurants and convenience stores divided by the number of grocery stores and supermarkets.

A standardized rating scale developed by Mahler et al in 1984, which is a general scale used to measure the changes in dyspnea severity from the baseline as established by the Baseline Dyspnea Index (BDI). The questionnaire contains 3 categories of 24 items change in functional impairment, change in magnitude of task, change in magnitude of effort. The instrument is scored on a seven-grade scale for each category, where grade -3 represents major deterioration and grade +3 represents major improvement.

preferred_term: Trauma Symptom Checklist for Children Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 54-item self-report questionnaire developed by Briere et al. in 1996, that utilizes a four-point Likert rating scale to evaluate severity of posttraumatic stress and related symptoms in children ages 10-17 years who have experienced traumatic events.

preferred_term: Trauma Symptom Checklist for Young Children Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 90-item caretaker-report questionnaire developed by Briere et al. in 1999, that utilizes a four-point Likert rating scale to evaluate severity of posttraumatic stress and related symptoms in children ages 3-12 years who have experienced traumatic events.
A 9 question subset of the Treatment Satisfaction Questionnaire for Medication that omits the five items of the side effects domain.

preferred_term: Treatment Satisfaction Questionnaire for Medication Version 1.4
definition: A standardized 14-item self-report scale developed by Atkinson et al in 2004 to assess the major dimensions of patients' satisfaction with medication. The instrument, which permits comparisons across medication types and patient conditions, provides scores on four scales: side effects, effectiveness, convenience, and global satisfaction.
A summation of the scores from multiple self-reported scales used to assess the physical and functional well-being of subjects in clinical trials.
A score derived from an assessment using a truth agreement scale.
The criteria developed by the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) for the classification and staging of neoplastic diseases.

synonyms: Mayo Clinical Index for Ulcerative Colitis|Mayo Score for Ulcerative Colitis|UCDAI|Ulcerative Colitis Disease Activity Index (UCDAI)
definition: A four-item instrument that utilizes a four-point rating scale to assess the severity of ulcerative colitis based on stool frequency, rectal bleeding, mucosal appearance at endoscopy, and physician rating of disease activity.
A score derived from an assessment using an undesired appetite increase scale.
A score derived from an assessment using an undesired sedation scale.

preferred_term: Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale 1999 Version Clinical Classification
definition: An updated and expanded version of the standardized clinician rating scale developed by the Huntington Study Group in 1996 for the assessment of motor function, cognitive function, behavioral abnormalities, and functional capacity in Huntington's Disease.

preferred_term: Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale Clinical Classification
definition: A standardized rating scale originally developed by S. Fahn, R. Elton, and other members of the UPDRS Development Committee in 1987 which is used to evaluate the status of a patient's Parkinson's disease. This instrument is made up of 6 parts consisting of a mental, behavior and mood section, an activities of daily living section, a motor examination, and questions concerning any complications with the patient's current therapy as well as modified versions of the Hoehn and Yahr Staging assessment and the Schwab and England Activities of Daily Living Scale. Each question is either scored with a 0 for a 'no' response and 1 for a 'yes' response or with a 0-4 point scale.

preferred_term: University of California San Francisco Cancer of the Prostate Risk Assessment Score
synonyms: CAPRA|Cancer of the Prostate Risk Assessment|UCSF Cancer of the Prostate Risk Assessment Score|UCSF-CAPRA
definition: A prostate cancer risk assessment score that utilizes pre-operative serum prostate specific antigen level, Gleason score, clinical tumor stage, biopsy results, and age to predict disease recurrence after radical prostatectomy.

preferred_term: University of North Carolina Genomic Knowledge Scale
synonyms: Genomic Knowledge Scale|UNC Genomic Knowledge Scale|UNC-GKS|University of North Carolina Genomic Knowledge Scale (UNC-GKS)
definition: A survey that healthcare providers can use to assess the relative advantage of a genomic intervention over their current practice.

preferred_term: University of Washington Quality of Life Scale, Version 4
definition: A questionnaire designed to assess health-related quality of life in head and neck cancer patients.

A standardized rating scale developed and published by Linda Cardozo, Karin Coyne and Eboo Versi in 2004 and copyrighted by Pfizer which is used to assess an individual's sense of urinary urgency. This instrument consists of one item in which the individual rates their experience when they have the desire to urinate. The item has three responses that receive a score of 0, 1, or 2.

preferred_term: VATS and Laparoscopy Surveillance Result Score
definition: A prospective scoring system related to suspicion of malignancy, including mesothelioma, as measured by video-assisted thoracoscopy and laparoscopy.
A four-item clinician-administered measure that utilizes a four-point rating scale to assess a patient's perception of dryness, soreness, irritation, and pain for their vaginal area.

preferred_term: Validated Hemophilia Regimen Treatment Adherence Scale - On-Demand
synonyms: VERITAS-PRN|Validated Hemophilia Regimen Treatment Adherence Scale - On-Demand (VERITAS-PRN)
definition: A standardized tool for evaluating adherence to episodic hemophilia treatment regimens. The tool consists of 24 questions presented as a 5-point Likert scale ranging from Always to Never. Total scores can range from 24 (most adherent) to 120 (least adherent), with subscores ranging from 1-4 in six categories: treat, time, dose, plan, remember, and communicate.

preferred_term: Validated Hemophilia Regimen Treatment Adherence Scale - Prophylaxis
synonyms: VERITAS-Pro|Validated Hemophilia Regimen Treatment Adherence Scale - Prophylaxis (VERITAS-Pro)
definition: A standardized tool for evaluating adherence to hemophilia prophylaxis, consisting of 24 questions utilizing 5-point Likert scales, with total scores ranging from 24 (most adherent) to 120 (least adherent). In addition to producing a total adherence score, questions are divided into six subscales (time, dose, plan, remember, skip, and communicate) to delineate the areas of nonadherence.

preferred_term: Validated Investigator Global Assessment scale for Atopic Dermatitis 2017
definition: A standardized, clinician-rated five-point rating scale, validated in 2017 by International Eczema Council, that is used to assess the global severity of atopic dermatitis.
A psychometric response scale to assess subjective characteristics or symptoms, which relies upon a scale of word values along a continuum in between two fixed end-points.

preferred_term: Veterans Administration Lung Study Group Clinical Classification
definition: A standardized two-stage classification system that was developed by the Veterans Administration Lung Study Group (VALG) in 1957 for the clinical staging of lung cancer as limited or extensive based on the anatomical scope of the disease.

preferred_term: Vignos Lower Extremity Rating Scale Clinical Classification
definition: A standardized 10-item clinician rating scale for the assessment of lower extremity function in patients diagnosed with neuromuscular disease. The scale was developed by Vignos, et al. in 1963 to evaluate ability and methods used for elevation and ambulation in patients with childhood progressive muscular dystrophy.

An instrument for evaluation of social functioning in individuals from preschool to 18 years old. Focusing on adaptive behaviors, the results are meant to support the diagnosis of intellectual and developmental disabilities.
A psychometric response scale to assess subjective characteristics or symptoms, which relies upon a continuum of possible result values in between two fixed end-points.
A score derived from an assessment using a visual analogue scale.

preferred_term: Visual Functioning Questionnaire 25-Interviewer Administered Format Version 2000
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by RAND and sponsored by the National Eye Institute (NEI) to assess the dimensions of self-reported vision-targeted health status that are most important for individuals with chronic eye disease. It also measures the influence of visual disability and symptoms on emotional well-being, mental health, social and daily visual functioning. This questionnaire is in the Interviewer Administered Format and contains 25 multiple choice questions across 3 domains; general health and vision, difficulty with activates, and response to vision problems. It also includes an appendix of additional 13 questions researchers can use to expand the scales up to 38 total items.

preferred_term: Visual Functioning Questionnaire 25-Self Administered Format Version 2000
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by RAND and sponsored by the National Eye Institute (NEI) to assess the dimensions of self-reported vision-targeted health status that are most important for individuals with chronic eye disease. It also measures the influence of visual disability and symptoms on emotional well-being, mental health, social and daily visual functioning. This questionnaire is in the Self or Patient Administered Format and contains 25 multiple choice questions across 3 domains; general health and vision, difficulty with activates, and response to vision problems. It also includes an appendix of additional 13 questions researchers can use to expand the scales up to 38 total items.
A subtest of the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale that measures nonverbal reasoning, and the ability to analyze and synthesize abstract visual stimuli.

A Visual Spatial subtest of the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children that measures the ability to analyze and synthesize information. Within a specified time limit, the child views a completed puzzle and selects three response options that, when combined, reconstruct the puzzle.
A composite score calculated from Weschler Memory Scale subtests that is used to assess visual working and visual-spatial working memory.
Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale, 4th Edition. The subject either names objects presented visually or defines words that are presented visually and orally.
A subtest of the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children, Fifth Edition. The subject either names objects presented visually or defines words that are presented visually and orally.
A subtest of the Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence, 4th Edition. Depending on the age of the child, this subtest measures either receptive vocabulary or verbal comprehension.
A subscale of the Voice Handicap Index that measures an individual's perception of the emotional impact of their voice handicap.
A subscale of the Voice Handicap Index that measures an individual's perception of the effect of their voice handicap on their daily activity.
A subscale of the Voice Handicap Index that measures an individual's perception of the characteristics of their vocal production and laryngeal discomfort.
A four-item clinician-administered measure that utilizes a four-point rating scale to assess a patient's perception of dryness, soreness, irritation, and pain for their vulvar area.
An index or composite score of the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale, fourth edition.

preferred_term: WHO Quality of Life Measure - Spirituality, Religion and Personal Beliefs
synonyms: Spirituality, Religiousness and Personal Beliefs (SRPB)|WHOQOL Spirituality, Religiousness and Personal Beliefs (SRPB) Field-Test Instrument|WHOQOL-SRPB|WHOQOL-SRPB Field Test Instrument|World Health Organization Quality of Life Measure, Spirituality, Religion and Personal Beliefs
definition: A questionnaire consisting of 32 questions, covering 8 quality of life aspects, that ask about an individual's spiritual, religious, or personal beliefs and how these beliefs affect their quality of life, including positive feelings, social support, self-esteem, pain, energy, work capacity, health and social care, and leisure activities.
An index or composite score of the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children, fifth edition.
An index or composite score of the Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence, fourth edition.
A questionnaire designed to measure a patient's opinion of their participation in a phase II or III treatment trial and their decision making regarding treatment.
A cognitive assessment tool that evaluates the ability to use spatial learning and recall using a T-shaped water maze.

synonyms: WMS-IV|Wechsler Memory Scale 4th Edition|Wechsler Memory Scale IV
definition: The fourth version of the Wechsler Memory Scale that was published in 2009. It is a neuropsychological test designed to measure different memory functions in individuals ranging in age from 16 to 90 years.

preferred_term: West Haven Criteria - West Haven Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade
synonyms: WHEG01-WH Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade|WHEG01-WH Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade|WHEG0101
definition: West Haven Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade (West Haven Criteria) Grade.

preferred_term: West Haven Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade Clinical Classification
synonyms: WEST HAVEN HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY GRADE|WHEG01|West Haven Criteria
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by Blei and Cordoba in 2001, which is a classification system used to describe the severity of hepatic encephalopathy based on the level of mental status and intellectual function. This instrument has 4 grades.

preferred_term: Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index Version 3.1 Likert Scale Format Questionnaire
synonyms: WOMAC Index Version 3.1 Likert Scale Format|WOMAC LK 3.1 INDEX|WOMAC1
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by Nicholas Bellamy with the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities in 1982, which is used to assess pain, stiffness, and physical function in patients with hip and/or knee osteoarthritis. The questionnaire contains 24 items which are divided into 3 subscales: pain, stiffness and physical function. This version of the questionnaire uses the 5-point Likert scaling format.

preferred_term: Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index Version 3.1 Numerical Rating Scale Format Questionnaire
synonyms: WOMAC Index Version 3.1 Numerical Rating Scale Format|WOMAC NRS 3.1 INDEX|WOMAC2
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by Nicholas Bellamy with the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities in 1982, which is used to assess pain, stiffness, and physical function in patients with hip and/or knee osteoarthritis. The questionnaire contains 24 items which are divided into 3 subscales: pain, stiffness and physical function. This version of the questionnaire uses the 11-point numerical rating scaling format.

preferred_term: Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index Version 3.1 Visual Analog Scale Format Questionnaire
synonyms: WOMAC Index Version 3.1 Visual Analog Scale Format|WOMAC VA 3.1 INDEX|WOMAC3
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by Nicholas Bellamy with the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities in 1982, which is used to assess pain, stiffness, and physical function in patients with hip and/or knee osteoarthritis. The questionnaire contains 24 items which are divided into 3 subscales: pain, stiffness and physical function. This version of the questionnaire uses the 100mm visual analog scaling format.
A rating scale included in the Behavior Assessment System for Children that measures the tendency of the subject to evade others to avoid social contact.
A comprehensive, individually administered cognitive assessment that identifies strengths and weaknesses in cognitive abilities, processes, and academic performance.

An instrument used in clinical occupational health and research to assess an individual's ability to work. It takes into consideration the demands of work, the worker's health status, and resources available to the individual.
A self-rated work ability scale that ranges from 0: Completely unable to work to 10: Best work ability at present.
A score derived from an assessment using a work ability index scale.

A standardized rating scale originally developed by Lerner et al in 1998. This instrument is used to measure the degree to which health problems interfere with specific aspects of job performance and health-related productivity loss in employed individuals.

preferred_term: Work Productivity and Activity Impairment General Health V2.0 Questionnaire
synonyms: WPAI-GH V2.0|WPAI-GH Version 2|WPAI02|WPAI:GH|Work Productivity and Activity Impairment General Health V2.0 Questionnaire (WPAI:GH)|Work Productivity and Activity Impairment General Health Version 2.0 Questionnaire
definition: The second version of a standardized survey originally developed by Margaret C. Reilly in 1993 which is used to evaluate the extent to which a patient's general health problems effects their ability to work and perform daily activities. This instrument consists of 6 questions which can be completed by the patient or in an interview format.

preferred_term: Work Productivity and Activity Impairment Specific Health Problems Version 2.0 Questionnaire
definition: The second version of a standardized survey originally developed by Margaret C. Reilly in 1993 which is used to evaluate the extent to which a patient's specific health problems effect their ability to work and perform daily activities. This instrument consist of 6 questions which can be completed by the patient or in an interview format.

A rating scale included in the Behavior Rating Inventory of Executive Function that measures the ability of an individual to hold information when completing a task, encoding information, or generating goals and plans in a sequential manner.

preferred_term: World Health Organization Clinical Staging of HIV/AIDS for Adults and Adolescents Clinical Classification
synonyms: WHIVS1|WHO Clinical Staging of HIV/AIDS for Adults and Adolescents|WHO HIV STAGING FOR ADULTS AND ADOLESCENTS
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by the World Health Organization (WHO) in 2007, which is a classification system used for immunological staging of HIV/AIDS in adults and adolescents with established HIV infection. This instrument classifies HIV disease on the basis of clinical manifestations.

preferred_term: World Health Organization Clinical Staging of HIV/AIDS for Children Clinical Classification
synonyms: WHIVS2|WHO Clinical Staging of HIV/AIDS for Children|WHO HIV STAGING FOR CHILDREN
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by the World Health Organization (WHO) in 2007, which is a classification system used for immunological staging of HIV/AIDS in children with established HIV infection. This instrument classifies HIV disease on the basis of clinical manifestations.

preferred_term: World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule 2.0 12+24-item Version Interviewer-administered Questionnaire
definition: A standardized interviewer-administered or computer-adaptive testing (CAT) questionnaire that utilizes a 5-point rating scale to assess an individual's ability to perform activities in six domains of functioning over the 30 days prior to evaluation in order to calculate a global disability score. The questionnaire is initially administered as 12 items to screen for problematic domains of functioning. Up to 24 items are then added based on the initial responses. The questionnaire was developed in 2010 by the World Health Organization (WHO) Classification, Terminology and Standards team, within the framework of the WHO/National Institutes of Health (NIH) Joint Project on Assessment and Classification of Disability.

preferred_term: World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule 2.0 12-item Version Interviewer-administered Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 12-item interviewer-administered questionnaire that utilizes a 5-point rating scale to provide a brief assessment of an individual's ability to perform activities in six domains of functioning over the 30 days prior to evaluation in order to calculate a global disability score. The questionnaire was developed in 2010 by the World Health Organization (WHO) Classification, Terminology and Standards team, within the framework of the WHO/National Institutes of Health (NIH) Joint Project on Assessment and Classification of Disability.

preferred_term: World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule 2.0 12-item Version Proxy-administered Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 12-item questionnaire, administered by a friend, relative, or caretaker, that utilizes a 5-point rating scale to provide a brief assessment of an individual's ability to perform activities in six domains of functioning over the 30 days prior to evaluation in order to calculate a global disability score. The questionnaire was developed in 2010 by the World Health Organization (WHO) Classification, Terminology and Standards team, within the framework of the WHO/National Institutes of Health (NIH) Joint Project on Assessment and Classification of Disability.

preferred_term: World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule 2.0 12-item Version Self-administered Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 12-item self-report questionnaire that utilizes a 5-point rating scale to provide a brief assessment of an individual's ability to perform activities in six domains of functioning over the 30 days prior to evaluation in order to calculate a global disability score. The questionnaire was developed in 2010 by the World Health Organization (WHO) Classification, Terminology and Standards team, within the framework of the WHO/National Institutes of Health (NIH) Joint Project on Assessment and Classification of Disability.

preferred_term: World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule 2.0 36-item Version Interviewer-administered Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 36-item interviewer-administered questionnaire that utilizes a 5-point rating scale to assess an individual's ability to perform activities in six domains of functioning over the 30 days prior to evaluation in order to calculate a global disability score. The questionnaire was developed in 2010 by the World Health Organization (WHO) Classification, Terminology and Standards team, within the framework of the WHO/National Institutes of Health (NIH) Joint Project on Assessment and Classification of Disability.

preferred_term: World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule 2.0 36-item Version Proxy-administered Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 36-item questionnaire, administered by a friend, relative, or caretaker, that utilizes a 5-point rating scale to assess an individual's ability to perform activities in six domains of functioning over the 30 days prior to evaluation in order to calculate a global disability score. The questionnaire was developed in 2010 by the World Health Organization (WHO) Classification, Terminology and Standards team, within the framework of the WHO/National Institutes of Health (NIH) Joint Project on Assessment and Classification of Disability.

preferred_term: World Health Organization Disability Assessment Schedule 2.0 36-item Version Self-administered Questionnaire
definition: A standardized 36-item self-report questionnaire that utilizes a 5-point rating scale to assess an individual's ability to perform activities in six domains of functioning over the 30 days prior to evaluation in order to calculate a global disability score. The questionnaire was developed in 2010 by the World Health Organization (WHO) Classification, Terminology and Standards team, within the framework of the WHO/National Institutes of Health (NIH) Joint Project on Assessment and Classification of Disability.

preferred_term: Young Mania Rating Scale Clinical Classification
definition: A standardized rating scale developed by R. C. Young and colleagues in 1978 to assess the severity of an individual's mania. This instrument contains 11 items or symptoms associated with mania which is administered by a clinician. Each item has 5 response options but the weight of each response differs with each item.

preferred_term: Young Mania Rating Scale Structured Interview Version Clinical Classification
synonyms: YMRS Interview Version|YMRS STRUCTURED INTERVIEW VERSION|YMRS02
definition: A standardized 11-item interviewer-administered assessment tool, published by Young et al in 1978, that utilizes five and nine point rating scales to assess the severity of mania.

preferred_term: iDworak et al Tumor Regression Grade (1997)
definition: A grading system developed by Dworak et al (1997) to assess a decrease in tumor size or extent in the body. (Dworak O, Keilholz L, Hoffmann A. Pathological features of rectal cancer after preoperative radiochemotherapy. Int J Colorectal Dis. (1997) 12:19-23.)
Complete resolution of thrombosis, with recanalization of vessel.

Any unequivocal, unambiguous increase in the volume of new or existing enhancing thrombosis. Unequivocal progression of thrombosis corresponds to at least 73% increase in volume, which is similar to 20% increase in diameter, and at least a 5 mm absolute increase.

Any of a) partial recanalization of thrombosis (if prior complete blockage), b) unequivocal reduction in the maximal girth of thrombosis, c) unequivocal reduction in the volume, or elimination, of arterial enhancing portion of thrombosis.

Any of a) no change or small changes that do not meet the criteria for irPR or irPD, taking as reference the smallest sum diameters while on study, b) increase in the volume of non-enhancing thrombosis, c) new bland non-enhancing thrombosis.
A score derived from an assessment using a zero to four agreement rating scale.
A score derived from an assessment using a one to six agreement rating scale.
A score derived from an assessment using a one to seven agreement rating scale.

AJCC Manual for Staging of Cancer 4th Edition (AJCC V4) (Beahrs OH, Henson DE, Hutter RVP, Kennedy BJ, editors. AJCC manual for staging of cancer (4th ed). Philadelphia, PA: J.B. Lippincott; 1992). The 6th edition of the criteria developed by the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) and used for the classification and staging of neoplastic diseases. It was published in 2002 and was in effect 2003-2009.
The 7th edition of the criteria developed by the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) in 2010, used for the classification and staging of neoplastic diseases.

synonyms: 8th|AJCC Ed. 8|AJCC Edition 8|AJCC V8|AJCC v8|AJCC2|AJCC8|American Joint Committee on Cancer Staging Manual, Edition 8|Eighth Edition (2017)
definition: The 8th edition of the criteria developed by the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC), implemented in 2018, used for the classification and staging of neoplastic diseases.

synonyms: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual Version 9|AJCC v9|AJCC9
definition: The 9th version of the criteria developed by the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) and used for the classification and staging of neoplastic diseases. Version 9 updates the Cervix Uteri Cancer Staging System and became effective on January 1, 2021. It replaces the 8th Edition Cervix Uteri cancer site content. All other disease sites in the 8th Edition Cancer Staging Manual remain current for 2021. With this iteration, the AJCC has changed how it will further update and release cancer staging content; it has shifted the naming for releases from "Cancer Staging Manual Edition" to "Cancer Staging System Version".
An eleven step Likert scale for ability that goes from 0: Cannot do at all to 100: Highly certain can do.
A score derived from an assessment using an ability Likert scale.
A 5-point Likert scale for agreement that goes from 1: Not at All to 5: Very Much.
A score derived from an assessment using an agreement Likert scale.
An anxiety scale from 0 to 10 on which the patient marks the current level of anxiety experienced.
A score derived from an assessment using an anxiety visual analogue scale.
A scale for the subjective scoring of fatigue interference that ranges from 0: Does Not Interfere to 10: Completely Interferes.
A scale for the subjective scoring of fatigue that ranges from 0: No Fatigue to 10: Fatigue As Bad As You Can Imagine.

parent_name: GvHD Body Surface Area Involvement Score
definition: No body surface area involved.

parent_name: GvHD Body Surface Area Involvement Score
definition: 1-18% of body surface area involved.

parent_name: GvHD Body Surface Area Involvement Score
definition: 19-50% of body surface area involved.

parent_name: GvHD Body Surface Area Involvement Score
definition: Greater than 50% of body surface area involved.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 0 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 1 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 10 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 2 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 3 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 4 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 5 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 6 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 7 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 8 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.

parent_name: Baseline Nausea and Vomiting Scale Score
definition: A subjective score of 9 on a scale that ranges from 0: No nausea at all to 10: Nausea as bad as it can be.
No pathologic changes.
Any stage of superficial punctate keratitis.
Epithelial opacities; micro-cysts; micro-deposits; corneal erosion; non-central stromal opacity.
Corneal ulcer without risk of acute rupture; central stromal opacity.
Corneal ulcer more severe than grade 3.
No findings, no reporting from the patient.
Symptomatic visual acuity loss of less than 3 lines. (ETDRS equivalent: each loss of 3 lines corresponds to halving the visual acuity)
Visual acuity loss greater than or equal to 3 lines, but less than 6 lines. (ETDRS equivalent: each loss of 3 lines corresponds to halving the visual acuity)
Visual acuity loss greater than or equal to 6 lines. (ETDRS equivalent: each loss of 3 lines corresponds to halving the visual acuity)
Visual acuity loss greater than or equal to 6 lines, leading to blindness. (ETDRS equivalent: each loss of 3 lines corresponds to halving the visual acuity)

synonyms: Pretty much bedridden, rarely out of bed|Score 4
definition: A response indicating that an individual is pretty much bedridden, rarely out of bed.

A Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology category that indicates that a cytopathology sample does not meet the minimum criteria for analysis, either due to an insufficient quantity of cells or excessive blood in the sample.
A Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology category that indicates a benign follicular nodule, lymphocytic (Hashimoto) thyroiditis, or granulomatous (subacute) thyroiditis.
A Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology category that indicates atypia of undetermined significance or a follicular lesion of undetermined significance.
A Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology category that indicates suspicion for follicular neoplasm. The nodule has a 23-34-% risk of being cancerous.
A Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology category that indicates suspicion of malignancy, with a 67-83% risk of being cancerous.
A Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology category that indicates malignancy, with a 97-100% risk of being cancerous.
Unprepared colon segment with mucosa not seen due to solid stool that cannot be cleared.
Portion of mucosa of the colon segment seen, but other areas of the colon segment not well seen due to staining, residual stool and/or opaque liquid.
Minor amount of residual staining, small fragments of stool and/or opaque liquid, but mucosa of colon segment seen well.
Entire mucosa of colon segment seen well with no residual staining, small fragments of stool or opaque liquid.
A subjective score of 0 on a rating scale that ranges from 0: Not at all bothered to 6: Extremely bothered.
A subjective score of 1 on a rating scale that ranges from 0: Not at all bothered to 6: Extremely bothered.
A subjective score of 2 on a rating scale that ranges from 0: Not at all bothered to 6: Extremely bothered.
A subjective score of 3 on a rating scale that ranges from 0: Not at all bothered to 6: Extremely bothered.
A subjective score of 4 on a rating scale that ranges from 0: Not at all bothered to 6: Extremely bothered.
A subjective score of 5 on a rating scale that ranges from 0: Not at all bothered to 6: Extremely bothered.
A subjective score of 6 on a rating scale that ranges from 0: Not at all bothered to 6: Extremely bothered.

A tool for the assessment of pain in both clinical and research settings. The BPI uses simple numeric rating scales from 0 to 10 that are easy to understand and easy to translate into other languages. On the BPI, mild pain is defined as a worst pain score of 1 - 4, moderate pain is defined as a worst pain score of 5 - 6, and severe pain is defined as a worst pain score of 7-10.
Separate hard lumps, hard to pass; indicative of severe constipation.
Lumpy and sausage-like; indicative of constipation.
A sausage shape with cracks in the surface; normal stool.
Like a smooth, soft sausage or snake; normal stool.

A Clinical Frailty Scale category that indicates that an individual is very fit. People who are robust, active, energetic, and motivated. These people commonly exercise regularly. They are among the fittest for their age.

A Clinical Frailty Scale category that indicates that an individual is fit or well. People who have no active disease symptoms but are less fit than category 1. Often, they exercise or are very active occasionally (e.g., seasonally).
A Clinical Frailty Scale category that indicates that an individual is managing well. People whose medical problems are well controlled but are not regularly active beyond routine walking.

A Clinical Frailty Scale category that indicates that an individual is living with very mild frailty. Previously denoted as "vulnerable", this category marks the transition from complete independence. Although not dependent on others for daily help, symptoms often limit activities. A common complaint is being "slowed up" or being tired during the day.

A Clinical Frailty Scale category that indicates that an individual is living with mild frailty. These people often have more evident slowing and need help in high-order instrumental activities of daily living (finances, transportation, heavy housework, medications). Typically, mild frailty progressively impairs shopping and walking outside alone, meal preparation, and housework.

A Clinical Frailty Scale category that indicates that an individual is living with moderate frailty. People need help with all outside activities and with keeping house. Inside, they often have problems with stairs and need help with bathing and might need minimal assistance (cueing, standby) with dressing.

A Clinical Frailty Scale category that indicates that an individual is living with severe frailty. Completely dependent for personal care, from whatever cause (physical or cognitive). Even so, they seem stable and not at high risk of dying (within about 6 months).

A Clinical Frailty Scale category that indicates that an individual is living with very severe frailty. Completely dependent, approaching the end of life. Typically, they could not recover even from a minor illness.

A Clinical Frailty Scale category that indicates that an individual is terminally ill. Approaching the end of life. This category applies to people with a life expectancy of less than 6 months, who are not otherwise evidently frail. Many terminally ill patients can still exercise until very close to death.

synonyms: FLIPI-2 Score 0|Follicular Lymphoma International Prognostic Index 2 Score 0

synonyms: FLIPI-2 Score 1-2|Follicular Lymphoma International Prognostic Index 2 Score 1-2

synonyms: FLIPI-2 Score 3-5|Follicular Lymphoma International Prognostic Index 2 Score 3-5
definition: A score indicating that a patient has 3-5 risk factors associated with follicular lymphoma and is considered in the high risk group.
A visual scale that uses facial expressions to help a person rate the intensity of certain sensations and feelings
A scale for the subjective scoring of fear that ranges from 1: Not at all afraid to 6: Very afraid.
A score derived from an assessment using a fear Likert scale.
A subjective score of 1 on a fit scale that ranges from 1: does not fit well to 5: fits very well.
A subjective score of 2 on a fit scale that ranges from 1: does not fit well to 5: fits very well.

A composite score of the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children that measures a child's ability to problem solve with unfamiliar information or while navigating new situations.

A composite score of the Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence that measures both fluid and inductive reasoning skills, broad visual intelligence, simultaneous processing, conceptual thinking, and classification abilities.

A composite score of the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale that is based on the total combined performance of the Verbal Comprehension Index, Perceptual Reasoning Index, Working Memory Index, and Processing Speed Index.

A composite score of the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children that is based on the total combined performance in five areas of cognitive ability: Verbal Comprehension, Visual-Spatial, Fluid Reasoning, Working Memory, and Processing Speed indexes.

A composite score of the Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence that represent a child's performance in five broad areas of cognitive ability. An index score of the Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence can range from 40 to 160, with score of the Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligences from 90 to 109 considered average.
A composite score of the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale that combines the six subtests that the Verbal Comprehension Index and Perceptual Reasoning Index comprise.
A 6-item, 5-point Likert scale used to assess patient satisfaction with genetic counseling.
Greater than 75% of tumor area shows glandular/tubular structures.
10% to 75% of tumor area shows glandular/tubular structures.
Less than 10% of tumor area shows glandular/tubular structures.
A score of 1 on the Combs Prognostic Index scale.

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease GvHD Final Lung Score 0|Score 0
definition: FEV1 is greater than or equal to 80 percent with or without lung symptoms.

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease GvHD Final Lung Score 1|Score 1
definition: FEV1 is 60 to 79 percent and lung symptoms are none or mild.

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease GvHD Final Lung Score 2|Score 2
definition: FEV1 is 40 to 59 percent and lung symptoms are none to moderate.

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease GvHD Final Lung Score 3|Score 3
definition: FEV1 is less than or equal to 39 percent and lung symptoms are none to severe.

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease Final Skin Score 0|Score 0
definition: Body surface area involvement is 0 percent and there are no sclerotic features.

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease Final Skin Score 1|Score 1
definition: Body surface area involvement is 1-18 percent.

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease Final Skin Score 2|Score 2
definition: Body surface area involvement is 19-50 percent and there are superficial sclerotic features.

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease Final Skin Score 3|Score 3
definition: Body surface area involvement is greater than 50 percent and there are any skin features that are Score 3. This score is also used when superficial sclerotic features are present (Score 2), but there is impaired mobility or ulceration (Score 3).

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease Lung Symptom Score 0|Score 0
definition: No symptoms.

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease Lung Symptom Score 1|Score 1
definition: Mild symptoms (shortness of breath after climbing one flight of steps).

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease Lung Symptom Score 2|Score 2
definition: Moderate symptoms (shortness of breath after walking on flat ground).

synonyms: Graft versus Host Disease Lung Symptom Score 3|Score 3
definition: Severe symptoms (shortness of breath at rest; requiring O2).

preferred_term: Hearing Environments and Reflection on Quality of Life Parent-proxy
parent_name: Hearing Environments and Reflection on Quality of Life
synonyms: HEAR-QL-Preschool|Hearing Environments and Reflection on Quality of Life Parent-proxy Measure|Hearing Environments and Reflection on Quality of Life Parent-proxy Measure for Preschool-aged Children|Hearing Environments and Reflection on Quality of Life Parent-proxy Measure for Preschool-aged Children (2-6 years old)|Parent-Proxy Preschool HEAR-QL|Preschool HEAR-QL
definition: A questionnaire designed to assess how a preschool child is affected by their hearing loss. Items are tailored to the experiences of preschool-aged children and administered as a parent-proxy (or caregiver) questionnaire.

preferred_term: Hearing Environments and Reflection on Quality of Life for Adolescents
parent_name: Hearing Environments and Reflection on Quality of Life
definition: A questionnaire designed to determine how an adolescent is affected by their hearing loss, and aid a clinician in determining where intervention is warranted to improve theadolescent's overall wellbeing.

preferred_term: Hearing Environments and Reflection on Quality of Life for Children
parent_name: Hearing Environments and Reflection on Quality of Life
definition: A questionnaire designed to assess how a child is affected by their hearing loss, and to aid a clinician in determining where intervention is warranted to improve the child's overall wellbeing.

preferred_term: High Risk Mantle Cell Lymphoma International Prognostic Index Combined Biologic Index
parent_name: Mantle Cell Lymphoma International Prognostic Index Combined Biologic Index
definition: High risk MIPI score (6-11) and Ki-67 greater than or equal to 30%. A MIPI-c high risk equals a MIPI-c score of 3.

preferred_term: High-Intermediate Risk Mantle Cell Lymphoma International Prognostic Index Combined Biologic Index
parent_name: Mantle Cell Lymphoma International Prognostic Index Combined Biologic Index
definition: Intermediate risk MIPI score (4-5) and Ki-67 greater than or equal to 30% or High risk MIPI score (6-11) and Ki-67 less than 30%. A MIPI-c high-intermediate risk equals a MIPI-c score of 2.
A score of 0 or 1 on the International Prognostic Index indicating that an individual has a low risk of progression of non-Hodgkin lymphoma and a 5 year survival of 73%.
A score of 2 on the International Prognostic Index indicating that an individual has a low-intermediate risk of progression of non-Hodgkin lymphoma and a 5 year survival of 69%.
A score of 3 on the International Prognostic Index indicating that an individual has a high-intermediate risk of progression of non-Hodgkin lymphoma and a 5 year survival of 46%.
A score of 4 or 5 on the International Prognostic Index indicating that an individual has a high risk of progression of non-Hodgkin lymphoma and a 5 year survival of 32%.

synonyms: IPSS Intermediate-2 Risk|Intermediate-2|International Prognostic Scoring System for Myelodysplastic Syndrome Intermediate-2 Risk Category

synonyms: IPSS Low Risk|International Prognostic Scoring System for Myelodysplastic Syndrome Low Risk Category|Low|Low Risk Myelodysplastic Syndrome|Low Risk Myelodysplastic Syndrome

synonyms: International Prognostic Scoring System for Myelodysplastic Syndrome Risk Score 0.5 to 1.0

synonyms: International Prognostic Scoring System for Myelodysplastic Syndrome Risk Score 1.5 to 2.0

synonyms: Complete Response|irCR|irCR (Immune-Related Response Criteria)|irRC Complete Response
definition: Complete disappearance of all lesions (whether measurable or not, and no new lesions); confirmation by a repeat, consecutive assessment no less than 4 weeks from the date first documented.

synonyms: Partial Response|irPR|irPR (Immune-Related Response Criteria)|irRC Criteria Partial Response
definition: Decrease in tumor burden greater than or equal to 50 percent relative to baseline confirmed by a consecutive assessment at least 4 weeks after first documentation.

synonyms: Progressive Disease|irPD|irPD (Immune-Related Response Criteria)|irRC Progressive Disease
definition: Increase in tumor burden greater than or equal to 25 percent relative to nadir (minimum recorded tumor burden); confirmation by a repeat, consecutive assessment no less than 4 weeks from the date first documented.

synonyms: Stable Disease|irRC Stable Disease|irSD|irSD (Immune-Related Response Criteria)
definition: Not meeting criteria for irCR or irPR, in absence of irPD.

synonyms: LOS|LOS Test|Limits of Stability Balance Test
definition: Any of various assessments designed to test an individual's ability to maintain upright body posture.

preferred_term: Little Activity; in Bed or Chair Most of Day
synonyms: Able to do little activity and spend most of the day in bed or chair|Score 3
definition: A response indicating that an individual is able to do little activity and spends most of the day in bed or chair.

parent_name: London Deauville Criteria Point Scale Score
synonyms: 1|London Deauville Criteria PET Point 1|PET Score 1|PET Score 1|Score 1
definition: The results of the positron emission tomography are negative; there is no detectable abnormal tracer uptake.

parent_name: London Deauville Criteria Point Scale Score
synonyms: 2|London Deauville Criteria PET Point 2|PET Score 2|PET Score 2|Score 2
definition: The results of the positron emission tomography are negative; the tracer uptake is less than or equal to the mediastinum.

parent_name: London Deauville Criteria Point Scale Score
synonyms: 3|London Deauville Criteria PET Point 3|PET Score 3|PET Score 3|Score 3
definition: The results of the positron emission tomography are negative; the tracer uptake is greater than the mediastinum but less than or equal to the liver.

parent_name: London Deauville Criteria Point Scale Score
synonyms: 4|London Deauville Criteria PET Point 4|PET Score 4|PET Score 4|Score 4
definition: The results of the positron emission tomography are positive; the tracer uptake is moderately more than the liver uptake, at any disease site.

parent_name: London Deauville Criteria Point Scale Score
synonyms: 5|London Deauville Criteria PET Point 5|PET Score 5|PET Score 5|Score 5
definition: The results of the positron emission tomography are positive; the tracer uptake is markedly increased at any disease site.

preferred_term: London Deauville Criteria Point Scale X
parent_name: London Deauville Criteria Point Scale Score
synonyms: London Deauville Criteria PET Point X|PET Score X|PET Score X|X
definition: New areas of uptake unlikely to be related to lymphoma.

preferred_term: Low Risk Mantle Cell Lymphoma International Prognostic Index Combined Biologic Index
parent_name: Mantle Cell Lymphoma International Prognostic Index Combined Biologic Index
definition: Low risk MIPI score (0-3) and Ki-67 less than 30%. Low risk MIPI-c equals a MIPI-c score of 0.

preferred_term: Low-Intermediate Risk Mantle Cell Lymphoma International Prognostic Index Combined Biologic Index
parent_name: Mantle Cell Lymphoma International Prognostic Index Combined Biologic Index
definition: Low risk MIPI score (0-3) and Ki-67 greater than or equal to 30% or Intermediate risk MIPI score (4-5) and Ki-67 less than 30%. MIPI-c low-intermediate risk equals a MIPI-c score of 1.

preferred_term: Lugano Lymphoma Response Classification Complete Remission by CT
definition: Target nodes/nodal masses must regress to less than or equal to 1.5 cm in the longest transverse diameter. Complete disappearance of radiologic evidence of disease. No extralymphatic sites of disease. Organ enlargement regress to normal. Bone marrow normal by morphology; if indeterminate, IHC negative.

preferred_term: Lugano Lymphoma Response Classification Complete Remission by PET
definition: A score of 1, 2, or 3 on a 5-point PET scale in nodal or extranodal sites with or without a residual mass. No evidence of FDG-avid disease in the marrow.

preferred_term: Lugano Lymphoma Response Classification No Response/Stable Disease by CT
definition: Less than 50 percent decrease from baseline in  the sum of the product of the perpendicular diameters of up to 6 dominant, measurable nodes and extranodal sites; no criteria for progressive disease are met.

preferred_term: Lugano Lymphoma Response Classification No Response/Stable Disease by PET
definition: A score of 4 or 5 on a 5-point PET scale with no significant change in FDG uptake from baseline, at interim or end of treatment. No change in the bone marrow from baseline.

preferred_term: Lugano Lymphoma Response Classification Partial Remission by CT
definition: For multiple lesions, greater than or equal to 50 percent decrease in the sum of the product of the perpendicular diameters of up to six target measurable nodes and extranodal sites. If only a single lesion is present, greater than or equal to 50 percent decrease in the cross product of the longest transverse diameter and perpendicular diameter.. Spleen must have regressed by >50% in length beyond normal" to the definition.

preferred_term: Lugano Lymphoma Response Classification Partial Remission by PET
definition: A score of 4 or 5 on a 5-point PET scale with reduced uptake compared with baseline and residual mass(es) of any size. Residual uptake higher than uptake in normal marrow but reduced compared to baseline.

preferred_term: Lugano Lymphoma Response Classification Progressive Disease by CT
definition: Progressive disease is assigned with at least one of the following: an individual node must be abnormal with the longest transverse diameter greater than 1.5 cm or an increase by greater than or equal to 50 percent from the product of the perpendicular diameter nadir; an increase in the longest transverse diameter or shortest transverse diameter from nadir 0.5 cm for lesions less than or equal to 2 cm or 1.0 cm for lesions greater than 2 cm. New or recurrent splenomegaly; a new node >1.5 cm in any axis; new or recurrent bone marrow involvement.

preferred_term: Lugano Lymphoma Response Classification Progressive Disease by PET
definition: A score of 4 or 5 on a 5-point PET scale with an increase in intensity of uptake from baseline and/or new FDG-avid foci consistent with lymphoma at interim or end of treatment assessment.
FEV1 greater than or equal to 80%.
FEV1 less than or equal to 39%.

synonyms: Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale (Pediatrics 10-18) Frequency Score 4 Point Scale
definition: A scale for the subjective scoring of event frequency that ranges from 1: Almost Never to 4: Almost Always designed for use with the Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale (MSAS) 10-18.

synonyms: MSAS 10-18|Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale (Pediatrics 10-18)|Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale 10-18
definition: A patient-rated questionnaire designed to evaluate the prevalence, characteristics, and distress to children with cancer aged 10-18. This 30-question instrument was adapted from a previously-validated adult version.

synonyms: MSAS 7-12|Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale (Pediatrics 7-12)|Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale 7-12
definition: A patient-rated questionnaire designed to evaluate the prevalence, characteristics, and distress to young children with cancer aged 7-12. This 8-question instrument was adapted from a previously-validated adult version.

preferred_term: Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale - Short Form
synonyms: MSAS-SF|Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale - Short Form - MSAS-SF
definition: An abbreviated version of the Memorial Symptom Assessment Scale designed to evaluate the burden of physical and psychological symptoms in advanced cancer patients.
A finding of less than or equal to 3 mitoses per mm2 (less than or equal to 7 per 10 hpf).
A finding of 4-7 mitoses per mm2 (8-14 per 10 hpf).
A finding of greater than or equal to 8 mitoses per mm2 (greater than 15 per 10 hpf).
A score of 2 on the Combs Prognostic Index scale.

A version of the Charlson Comorbidity Index that is modified for a specific disease by removing specific conditions to better fit specific populations. For example, kidney disease components may be removed for hemodialysis patients to prevent the skewing of the data.
No organ involvement; absence of acute graft-versus-host disease (aGvHD).
Mild skin involvement (severity stage 1 or 2), without liver or gastrointestinal (GI) involvement.
Moderate involvement of skin (severity stage 3) with mild liver or gastrointestinal (GI) involvement (severity stage 1).
Moderate involvement of liver and/or gastrointestinal (GI) system (severity stage 2 or 3), or more severe GI involvement (severity stage 3 or 4).
Severe involvement of skin, liver, or gastrointestinal (GI) system (severity stage 4).

synonyms: MOCA v8.1|Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) version 8.1|Montreal Cognitive Assessment, v8.1
definition: The version of the Montreal Cognitive Assessment that added the Memory Index Score to the MoCA test. It contains additional scoring precisions and replaced version 7.1.

synonyms: MOCA v8.2|Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) version 8.2|Montreal Cognitive Assessment, v8.2
definition: An alternate version of MOCA v8 that can be administered to decrease any potential test re-test interactions.

synonyms: MOCA v8.3|Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) version 8.3|Montreal Cognitive Assessment, v8.3
definition: An alternate version of MOCA v8 that can be administered to decrease any potential test re-test interactions.
A scale for the subjective scoring of an individual's motivation that ranges from 1: Not at all motivated to 7: Extremely motivated.
A test designed to evaluate the effectiveness of a patient's motor automatic reflex responses to restore balance following sudden, unexpected forward and backward perturbations of a support surface.

parent_name: Pediatric Quality of Life Brain Tumor Module Subscale
definition: A subscale of the Pediatric Quality of Life Brain Tumor Module designed to assess issues associated with movement and balance.

preferred_term: NCCN Prostate Cancer Favorable-Intermediate Risk Group
parent_name: NCCN Prostate Cancer Intermediate Risk Group
definition: A prostate cancer risk group characterized by: Gleason score of 3 + 4 or less, positive biopsy cores less than 50%, and at most 1 determinant of intermediate risk.

preferred_term: NCCN Prostate Cancer Unfavorable-Intermediate Risk Group
parent_name: NCCN Prostate Cancer Intermediate Risk Group
definition: A prostate cancer risk group characterized by: primary Gleason pattern 4, positive biopsy cores greater than or equal to 50%, or multiple determinants of intermediate risk.

preferred_term: NIH Consensus Response Criteria Mild Chronic GVHD
parent_name: NIH Consensus Response Criteria for Chronic GVHD Severity Score

preferred_term: NIH Consensus Response Criteria Moderate Chronic GVHD
parent_name: NIH Consensus Response Criteria for Chronic GVHD Severity Score

preferred_term: NIH Consensus Response Criteria Severe Chronic GVHD
parent_name: NIH Consensus Response Criteria for Chronic GVHD Severity Score
definition: At least 1 organ with a score of 3, or a lung score of 2 or 3.

preferred_term: Short Form 36 Health Survey Acute, US Version 1.0 Questionnaire
definition: A standardized, multipurpose, general health survey developed by John E. Ware and copyrighted by the Medical Outcomes Trust and Quality Metric Inc. in 1988 and 2002, which is used to assess the patient's functional health and overall well-being. This first version contains 36 questions that refer to the patient's symptoms or status within the past week and can be completed by the patient. For each question the patient must choose from five different responses.

synonyms: WISC|WISC-III|WISC-IV|Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children, Fourth Edition|Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children, Third Edition
definition: An individually administered clinical instrument for assessing the cognitive ability of children aged 6 years through 16 years 11 months. It is a psychometric test that assesses a child's current cognitive abilities in both verbal and nonverbal areas, including verbal comprehension, perceptual reasoning, working memory, and processing speed. It was first published in 1949 and revised in 1974 (WISC-R). The third edition (WISC-III) was released in 1991 and the fourth edition (WISC-IV) in 2003.

preferred_term: Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children - Fifth Edition
synonyms: WISC-V|Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children 5th Edition|Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children V

It is an individually administered clinical instrument for assessing the cognitive ability of children aged 6 years through 16 years 11 months. It is a psychometric test that assesses a child's current cognitive abilities in both verbal and nonverbal areas, including verbal comprehension, perceptual reasoning, working memory, and processing speed.

preferred_term: Wechsler Preschool and Primary Scale of Intelligence
definition: An intelligence test published by Harcourt Assessment and designed for children ages 2 years 6 months to 7 years 3 months. It provides subtest and composite scores that represent intellectual functioning in verbal and performance cognitive domains, and provides a composite score that represents a child's general intellectual ability.

parent_name: West Haven Criteria - West Haven Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade
definition: Minimal - symptoms or pathological characteristics include minimal hepatic encephalopathy, lack of detectable changes in personality or behavior, minimal changes in memory, concentration and intellectual function, and absence of coordination asterixis. (Adapted from: Hepatic Encephalopathy. David C Wolf. http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/186101-overview)

parent_name: West Haven Criteria - West Haven Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade
definition: Mild - symptoms or pathological characteristics include trivial lack of awareness, shortened attention span, impaired ability to perform addition or subtraction, hypersomnia, insomnia, or inversion of sleep pattern, euphoria, depression or irritability, mild confusion, slowing of ability to perform mental tasks. (Adapted from: Hepatic Encephalopathy. David C Wolf. http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/186101-overview.)

parent_name: West Haven Criteria - West Haven Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade
definition: Moderate - symptoms or pathological characteristics include lethargy or apathy, disorientation, inappropriate behavior, slurred speech, obvious asterixis, drowsiness, lethargy, gross deficits in ability to perform mental tasks, obvious personality changes, inappropriate behavior, and intermittent disorientation, unsurely regarding time. (Adapted from: Hepatic Encephalopathy. David C Wolf. http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/186101-overview.)

parent_name: West Haven Criteria - West Haven Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade
definition: Severe - symptoms or pathological characteristics include somnolence with some arousability, inability to perform mental tasks, disorientation about time and place, marked confusion, amnesia, occasional fits of rage, present but incomprehensible speech. (Adapted from: Hepatic Encephalopathy. David C Wolf. http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/186101-overview.)

parent_name: West Haven Criteria - West Haven Hepatic Encephalopathy Grade
definition: Coma - symptoms or pathological characteristics include coma with or without response to painful stimuli. (Adapted from: Hepatic Encephalopathy. David C Wolf. http://emedicine.medscape.com/article/186101-overview.)
